questions.question: In the evidence hierarchy for dietary changes that impact mental health, what is ranked as the top priority?

options.text: Taking psychobiotic supplements
Reducing ultra-processed food intake
Adding fermented foods to your diet
Following a specific named diet plan exactly

questions.explanation: Reducing ultra-processed food intake is the top priority because it has 'convincing' evidence (the highest rating) linking it to 53% higher odds of common mental disorders. UPFs drive inflammation, damage the gut barrier, displace essential nutrients, and hijack reward circuitry — reducing them addresses multiple harmful pathways simultaneously.

questions.question: What does research show about making 1-2 dietary changes at a time versus comprehensive dietary overhauls?

options.text: Comprehensive overhauls produce better results in both short and long term
Gradual change (1-2 changes maintained for 2-4 weeks each) produces significantly higher long-term adherence with similar total improvement at 12 months
The number of simultaneous changes does not matter
You should make all changes within the first week or not bother at all

questions.explanation: Research shows that participants who made 1-2 changes at a time, maintaining each for 2-4 weeks before adding new ones, had significantly higher long-term adherence (12+ months) compared to those attempting comprehensive overhauls. The gradual approach resulted in similar total improvement at 12 months but with far less dropout and psychological distress.

questions.question: What does the dose-response principle across all dietary pattern research mean for someone whose diet is far from ideal?

options.text: They need to achieve a perfect diet before any benefits appear
There is no minimum threshold — any incremental improvement in dietary pattern benefits mental health
Benefits only appear after six months of strict adherence
They should focus exclusively on supplements since diet change takes too long

questions.explanation: The dose-response relationship is consistent across all evidence reviewed in the course — UPF reduction, Mediterranean adherence, DII improvement, and psychobiotic supplementation. There is no threshold effect, meaning every incremental improvement matters. A person who moves from 60% UPF to 45% and increases MEDAS from 4 to 7 will likely experience meaningful improvements even though their diet is far from 'ideal.'

questions.question: What is the 80/20 guideline for sustainable dietary change?

options.text: Eat 80% of your food in the morning and 20% at night
Spend 80% of your food budget on organic foods and 20% on conventional
Aim for 80% of food choices to align with your plan, with 20% flexibility for social meals, celebrations, and comfort food
Eat only 80% of each meal and leave 20% on the plate

questions.explanation: The 80/20 guideline means aiming for 80% of your food choices to align with your evidence-based plan, with the remaining 20% for flexibility — social meals, celebrations, comfort food, travel, or days when your plan is not feasible. This is not cheating; it is sustainability built into the design, preventing the all-or-nothing thinking that derails dietary change.

questions.question: Why does the sustainability principle 'add before you subtract' work better than restriction-focused approaches?

options.text: Because adding foods is cheaper than removing them
Because adding nourishing foods naturally crowds out less healthy options without creating feelings of deprivation
Because you can never actually remove foods from your diet
Because adding foods increases your calorie intake, which improves mood

questions.explanation: Focusing on adding nourishing foods rather than restricting others works because it naturally crowds out less healthy options. For example, adding a large salad before dinner naturally reduces how much processed food you eat — without a rule telling you not to eat it. This avoids the deprivation mindset that often leads to abandoning dietary changes.

title: Building Your Evidence-Based Dietary Pattern - Quiz
